cards.text: The {{c1::=}} operator is called the {{c2::assignment}} operator.

cards.question: What value does variable x contain after these statements execute?
cards.explanation: First x is defined (uninitialized), then assigned 5, then assigned 10. The second assignment overwrites the first value.

cards.front: What is the difference between initialization and assignment?
cards.back: Initialization provides a variable with an initial value at the point of definition. Assignment gives a variable a value after it has already been defined.

cards.text: There are 6 basic ways to initialize variables in C++: default, copy, direct, list ({{c1::brace}}), value, and zero initialization.
List initialization (brace initialization): {{c1::int width{5};}} or {{c2::int width = {5};}}

cards.concept: Why list initialization (brace initialization) is preferred
cards.explanation:
List initialization (also called uniform initialization or brace initialization) is the modern C++ way to initialize variables:
  • Works for all initialization types
  • Disallows narrowing conversions (prevents data loss)
  • Prevents certain types of unexpected behavior
examples: int y = 5;     // Okay but older style

cards.text: Value initialization (zero initialization): {{c1::int width{};}} initializes the variable to {{c2::zero}} (or empty).

cards.front: When should you initialize a variable?
cards.back: Initialize a variable upon creation whenever possible. If you don't yet know what value the variable should have, use value initialization (empty braces) to initialize it to zero.

cards.question: What's the problem with this code?
cards.code:
int a, b = 5;
int c, d{6};
cards.answer: Only b and d are initialized; a and c are uninitialized
cards.explanation: When defining multiple variables on one line, each variable must be initialized separately. The initialization only applies to the variable immediately before the initializer.

cards.front: What should you do with an unused variable in your code?
cards.back: Remove it. Unused variables clutter code, may indicate bugs, and modern compilers will warn about them. There's no reason to keep them in the code.
